subAreas.name: Upper Trapezius
subAreas.description: Top of shoulders, near the base of the neck

exercises.name: Resistance-Band Shrug
exercises.mechanism: Strengthens the upper trapezius through full range, normalizing chronic low-grade tonic activation from prolonged sitting. Andersen et al. demonstrated that strengthening reduces upper-trap myalgia.
instructions: Stand on the middle of a long band, feet hip-width apart.
Hold the ends at your sides, arms straight, palms facing in.
Without bending your elbows, lift both shoulders straight up toward your ears.
Pause 1 second at the top.
Lower under control over 2 seconds.
exercises.bandTensionNote: Start red, progress to green or blue as 12 reps become easy
commonMistakes: Rolling shoulders (this is a vertical lift only)
contraindications: Acute cervical radiculopathy with arm numbness
Recent whiplash (less than 2 weeks)
Uncontrolled hypertension
exercises.beginnerModification: Bodyweight shrugs (no band) for the first 1-2 weeks, or seated shrugs with a yellow band.
exercises.evidenceFull: Andersen LL, Saervoll CA, Mortensen OS, et al. Effectiveness of small daily amounts of progressive resistance training for frequent neck/shoulder pain. Pain. 2011;152(2):440-446.
exercises.evidenceSummary: Randomized controlled trial of 198 office workers with frequent neck/shoulder pain. Just 2 minutes per day of elastic-tubing exercises, 5 days/week for 10 weeks, significantly reduced pain compared to the control group.

subAreas.name: Levator Scapulae
subAreas.description: Side of the neck, running from the upper neck to the inner edge of the shoulder blade

exercises.name: Banded Levator Scapulae Stretch
exercises.targetMuscles: Levator scapulae (passive lengthening) and scapular depressors (active)
exercises.mechanism: Forward-head posture creates sustained levator scapulae over-activation. Anchoring the scapula in depression with the band allows true muscle elongation rather than compensatory scapular elevation.
instructions: Sit tall and step on a band with your left foot.
Hold the band in your left hand at your side, arm long — this depresses the left scapula.
Turn your head 45 degrees to the right (look toward the right armpit).
Gently tuck your chin.
Place your right hand on the back of your head and apply mild downward assistance.
Hold 30 seconds, repeat 2-3 times, then switch sides.
exercises.bandTensionNote: Yellow or red — light is sufficient; the band anchors, doesn't load
commonMistakes: Letting the anchored shoulder shrug up
Pulling hard on the head — use only the weight of your hand
contraindications: Acute cervical disc herniation
Vertebrobasilar insufficiency symptoms (dizziness, visual changes with neck motion)
exercises.beginnerModification: Without a band, simply grab the seat of your chair on the anchor side.
exercises.evidenceFull: Sihawong R, Janwantanakul P, Sitthipornvorakul E, Pensri P. Exercise therapy for office workers with nonspecific neck pain: a systematic review. J Manipulative Physiol Ther. 2011;34(1):62-71.
exercises.evidenceSummary: Systematic review of exercise therapy for office worker neck pain. Stretching is helpful as an adjunct but is more effective when combined with strengthening.

subAreas.name: Cervical Paraspinals & Sub-Occipital
subAreas.description: Back of the neck and base of the skull

exercises.name: Resistance-Band Chin Tuck
exercises.targetMuscles: Deep cervical flexors (longus colli, longus capitis)
exercises.mechanism: People with chronic neck pain show reduced deep cervical flexor (DCF) activity and compensatory overuse of superficial flexors. Strengthening DCFs improves neuromuscular coordination, head posture, and pain.
instructions: Sit or stand tall.
Loop a long band behind your upper neck/occiput.
Hold the two ends in front at chest height, arms forward at 90 degrees to anchor.
Without tilting, draw your chin straight back ('double chin') — a horizontal slide.
Hold 5 seconds.
Slowly release.
exercises.bandTensionNote: Yellow or red only — heavier loads cause superficial flexor recruitment
commonMistakes: Nodding head down — should be horizontal translation
contraindications: Recent cervical surgery
Rheumatoid arthritis with atlanto-axial instability
Severe spinal stenosis with myelopathic signs
exercises.beginnerModification: Supine chin tuck on the floor with no band — gravity provides enough resistance.
exercises.evidenceFull: Jull GA, Falla D, Vicenzino B, Hodges PW. The effect of therapeutic exercise on activation of the deep cervical flexor muscles in people with chronic neck pain. Man Ther. 2009;14(6):696-701.
exercises.evidenceSummary: Targeted deep cervical flexor training improved muscle activation patterns and reduced pain in chronic neck pain patients compared to general strengthening.

subAreas.name: Rotator Cuff
subAreas.description: Deep shoulder stabilizers (supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, subscapularis)

exercises.name: Band Shoulder External Rotation
exercises.mechanism: External rotators are postural antagonists to the internally-rotated, protracted desk posture. Strengthening them improves the external/internal rotation ratio and dynamic glenohumeral centering.
instructions: Anchor a band at elbow height (a doorknob works).
Stand with the working side away from the anchor.
Tuck a rolled towel between your elbow and ribs.
Bend the elbow to 90 degrees and grasp the band across your body.
Rotate your forearm outward, pivoting at the shoulder, while keeping the elbow tucked.
Pause at end-range, then return slowly over 3-4 seconds.
exercises.bandTensionNote: Yellow, red, then green progression — very light loads
commonMistakes: Elbow drifting away from the side
Using a band so heavy the deltoid takes over
contraindications: Full-thickness rotator cuff tear not yet evaluated
Suspected labral tear with mechanical locking
Less than 6 weeks post-shoulder surgery without surgeon clearance
exercises.beginnerModification: Sub-maximal isometric ER (push fist into a doorframe) for acute pain — isometrics produce analgesia in rotator cuff related shoulder pain.
exercises.evidenceFull: Boudreau N, Gaudreault N, Roy JS, Bedard S, Balg F. The addition of glenohumeral adductor coactivation to a rotator cuff exercise program for rotator cuff tendinopathy: a single-blind randomized controlled trial. J Orthop Sports Phys Ther. 2019;49(3):126-135.
exercises.evidenceSummary: Adding adductor coactivation (towel between elbow and ribs) to rotator cuff exercises improved outcomes in patients with rotator cuff tendinopathy.

exercises.name: Banded Lateral Raise
exercises.mechanism: Direct exercise from Andersen's Copenhagen protocol. Reduced neck and shoulder pain in office workers in a 1-year RCT.
instructions: Stand on the band, feet hip-width apart.
Arms at your sides, palms in, slight elbow bend.
Raise your arms out to the sides until parallel with the floor — no higher (avoids the impingement zone).
Pause briefly.
Lower under control over 3 seconds.
contraindications: Active subacromial pain syndrome flare — do external rotation first
Painful arc 60-120 degrees that has not been evaluated
exercises.beginnerModification: Raise in the scapular plane (about 30 degrees forward of frontal) for less impingement.
exercises.evidenceFull: Andersen LL, Kjaer M, Sogaard K, Hansen L, Kryger AI, Sjogaard G. Effect of two contrasting types of physical exercise on chronic neck muscle pain. Arthritis Rheum. 2008;59(1):84-91.
exercises.evidenceSummary: Specific strength training including lateral raises significantly reduced neck and shoulder pain in office workers compared to general fitness training.

subAreas.name: Scapular Stabilizers
subAreas.description: Muscles between the shoulder blades (rhomboids, mid/lower trap, serratus anterior)

exercises.name: Resistance-Band Face Pull
exercises.targetMuscles: Posterior deltoid, infraspinatus, teres minor, middle and lower trapezius, rhomboids
exercises.mechanism: Combines horizontal abduction with external rotation — the exact pattern reversed by desk posture. Increases lower-trap activity and scapular posterior tilt.
instructions: Anchor the band at forehead height — this allows for external rotation, not just a row.
Grasp the band with palms down, thumbs out.
Step back to create light tension.
Pull the band toward your forehead, elbows high and wide.
Externally rotate so your hands finish on either side of your head (a '90/90' finish).
Squeeze for 1 second, then return slowly.
commonMistakes: Anchor too low — becomes a row, no external rotation
Pulling with arms instead of scapulae
contraindications: Active rotator cuff tendinopathy with a painful arc — start with isometric external rotation first
exercises.beginnerModification: Half-kneeling face pull to remove low-back compensation.
exercises.evidenceShort: Cools AM, Am J Sports Med 2007
exercises.evidenceFull: Cools AM, Dewitte V, Lanszweert F, et al. Rehabilitation of scapular muscle balance: which exercises to prescribe? Am J Sports Med. 2007;35(10):1744-1751.
exercises.evidenceSummary: EMG study identifying exercises that produce optimal balance between scapular stabilizers. Face pull-style movements produce high lower-trap and middle-trap activation with low upper-trap involvement.

exercises.name: Seated Resistance-Band Row
exercises.targetMuscles: Rhomboids, middle and lower trapezius, posterior deltoid, latissimus dorsi
exercises.mechanism: Strengthens scapular retractors to counteract the protraction-dominant desk posture and reduce interscapular ache.
instructions: Sit on the floor or a chair. Anchor a band at chest height in front (or loop it under your feet for a seated floor version).
Hold the ends with arms straight.
Pull your elbows back close to your ribs while squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause.
Return over 3 seconds.
exercises.bandTensionNote: Red, then green, then blue as strength builds
commonMistakes: Flexing the lumbar spine to 'throw' the band
contraindications: Acute cervical radiculopathy producing interscapular pain — treat the neck first
exercises.beginnerModification: Isometric row holds (3 x 20 seconds) if motion is provocative.
exercises.evidenceFull: Ekstrom RA, Donatelli RA, Soderberg GL. Surface electromyographic analysis of exercises for the trapezius and serratus anterior muscles. J Orthop Sports Phys Ther. 2003;33(5):247-258.
exercises.evidenceSummary: EMG study showing unilateral rowing produces high middle and lower trapezius activation (45% MVIC), making it effective for scapular retraction strengthening.

subAreas.name: Mid-Back (Thoracic Spine)
subAreas.description: The middle of the back, between the ribs

exercises.name: Banded Y-T-W
exercises.targetMuscles: Lower trapezius (Y), middle trapezius (T), rhomboids and posterior cuff (W)
exercises.mechanism: Activates the lower and middle trapezius in their primary lines of action, restoring scapular balance disrupted by prolonged sitting.
instructions: Anchor a band at floor level.
Y: Hold the ends with arms overhead at 30 degrees forward of frontal, thumbs up. Raise to form a Y with your body, squeezing your lower scapulae.
T: Arms straight out to the sides at shoulder height, thumbs up. Pull back, squeezing your mid-back.
W: Elbows at 90 degrees, hands up. Pull elbows down and back, externally rotating ('goal-post' finish).
commonMistakes: Shrugging during the Y — defeats lower-trap focus
contraindications: Recent thoracic compression fracture
Severe kyphosis with balance issues
exercises.beginnerModification: Bodyweight only ('floor angels') if shoulder range of motion is limited.
exercises.evidenceFull: Castelein B, Cagnie B, Parlevliet T, Cools A. Optimal normalization tests for muscle activation of the levator scapulae, pectoralis minor, and rhomboid major: an electromyography study using maximum voluntary isometric contractions. J Athl Train. 2016;51(8):671-677.
exercises.evidenceSummary: Systematic comparison of scapular exercises. The prone Y produces the optimal upper-to-lower-trap activation ratio, making it effective for correcting scapular imbalance.

exercises.name: Banded Hip Hinge (Good Morning)
exercises.mechanism: Teaches dissociated hip flexion with a neutral spine — the core skill missing in desk workers whose lumbar erectors are deconditioned from prolonged flexed sitting.
instructions: Stand on the band.
Loop the other end over the back of your neck/shoulders (with a towel for padding) or hold it at clavicle level.
Soft knees, neutral spine.
Hinge at the hips, pushing them back, until your torso is about 45 degrees (not parallel).
Drive hips forward to stand tall. The band tries to pull you forward — your erectors resist isometrically.
exercises.bandTensionNote: Red, then green — modest load; this is a movement-quality drill, not a max-strength lift
contraindications: Acute disc herniation with radiculopathy
Spondylolisthesis grade II or above
Less than 6 weeks post-spinal surgery
exercises.beginnerModification: Wall hinge — stand with back to the wall and slide your hips back to learn the pattern.
exercises.evidenceFull: Owen PJ, Miller CT, Mundell NL, et al. Which specific modes of exercise training are most effective for treating low back pain? Network meta-analysis. Br J Sports Med. 2020;54(21):1279-1287.
exercises.evidenceSummary: Network meta-analysis ranking exercise modes for low back pain. Resistance and motor-control training were among the most effective for reducing pain and disability.

subAreas.name: Wrist (Palm Side) / Carpal Tunnel
subAreas.description: Front of the wrist and base of the palm

exercises.name: Median Nerve Glide
exercises.targetMuscles: Median nerve excursion through the carpal tunnel
exercises.mechanism: Mobilizes the nerve relative to surrounding tissue, improves intraneural blood flow, and reduces edema and symptoms.
instructions: Position 1: Make a fist with the thumb tucked inside.
Position 2: Open and extend all fingers and the thumb.
Position 3: Extend the wrist with the thumb still tucked.
Position 4: Extend the thumb.
Position 5: Supinate the forearm (palm up) and apply a gentle thumb stretch with the other hand.
Hold each position 3-5 seconds and flow smoothly between them.
exercises.bandTensionNote: No band required for the glide itself
commonMistakes: Overstretching into a nerve 'twang' — pins-and-needles down the arm means back off
contraindications: Acute traumatic nerve injury
Symptoms peripheralize during the glide
Pregnancy-related carpal tunnel — consult a clinician
exercises.beginnerModification: Tendon glides only (the 5-position fist sequence) — gentler than nerve mobilization.
exercises.evidenceFull: Ballestero-Perez R, Plaza-Manzano G, Urraca-Gesto A, et al. Effectiveness of nerve gliding exercises on carpal tunnel syndrome: a systematic review. J Manipulative Physiol Ther. 2017;40(1):50-59.
exercises.evidenceSummary: Systematic review of 13 trials. Nerve gliding exercises combined with conservative care improved symptom severity and function in carpal tunnel syndrome.

subAreas.name: Wrist (Back of Hand)
subAreas.description: Back of the wrist and forearm

exercises.name: Banded Wrist Extension
exercises.targetMuscles: Extensor carpi radialis brevis/longus, extensor digitorum
exercises.mechanism: One of the five Andersen Copenhagen exercises. Protects against keyboard and mouse-related lateral elbow tendinopathy.
instructions: Sit and rest your forearm on your thigh, palm down, with your hand off the knee.
Step on the band and grasp it with the affected hand.
Lift the back of your hand up against resistance over 2 seconds.
Lower over 3 seconds.
commonMistakes: Using fingers instead of the wrist
contraindications: Acute lateral epicondylitis flare — use isometric holds only
exercises.beginnerModification: Isometric wrist extension against the other hand for 5-second holds, 10 reps.
exercises.evidenceFull: Andersen LL, Saervoll CA, Mortensen OS, et al. Effectiveness of small daily amounts of progressive resistance training for frequent neck/shoulder pain. Pain. 2011;152(2):440-446.
exercises.evidenceSummary: RCT of 198 office workers. Just 2 minutes per day of elastic-tubing exercises, including wrist extension, significantly reduced pain over 10 weeks.

exercises.name: Tyler Twist (Eccentric Wrist Extension)
exercises.targetMuscles: Wrist extensors at the common extensor tendon origin
exercises.mechanism: Eccentric loading promotes collagen remodeling and reduces neovascularization in chronic tendinopathy. Tyler et al. reported 81% pain reduction in the FlexBar group vs 22% in standard care.
instructions: Hold a FlexBar vertically in front of you with the affected arm, palm toward you, wrist in maximum extension.
Grasp the top of the bar with the other hand, palm away.
Twist the top hand toward you (away from your face) while keeping the affected wrist in extension.
Extend both arms straight out — the affected wrist now points down, the uninjured wrist points up.
Slowly un-twist over 4 seconds, letting the affected wrist flex against the bar's resistance.
exercises.tempo: Eccentric phase 4 seconds, 30 seconds rest between sets
exercises.bandTensionNote: Women start with the red FlexBar (10 lb), men with green (15 lb). If no FlexBar, substitute eccentric-biased banded wrist extension.
commonMistakes: Concentric loading on the affected side
Moving too fast through the eccentric phase
contraindications: Acute full-thickness extensor tendon rupture
Recent steroid injection (less than 6 weeks — increased rupture risk)
Skin breakdown over the elbow
exercises.beginnerModification: Banded wrist extension with heavy eccentric bias (4-second lower) if no FlexBar.
exercises.evidenceFull: Tyler TF, Thomas GC, Nicholas SJ, McHugh MP. Addition of isolated wrist extensor eccentric exercise to standard treatment for chronic lateral epicondylosis: a prospective randomized trial. J Shoulder Elbow Surg. 2010;19(6):917-922.
exercises.evidenceSummary: Randomized trial in chronic tennis elbow. The Tyler Twist group achieved 81% pain reduction and 76% DASH improvement vs 22% and 13% in the standard-care group over 7 weeks.

exercises.name: Reverse Tyler Twist
exercises.targetMuscles: Wrist flexor-pronator group at the common flexor tendon
exercises.mechanism: Same eccentric tendon remodeling principle as the Tyler Twist, applied to the flexor side. Tyler et al. 2014 showed it effective in patients who had failed prior conservative care.
instructions: Mirror of the Tyler Twist: affected arm in maximum wrist flexion.
Twist with the uninjured hand to pre-load the bar.
Slowly extend the affected wrist eccentrically over 4 seconds.
commonMistakes: Concentric loading on the affected side
contraindications: Ulnar neuritis with paresthesia in the 4th and 5th fingers — consult a clinician
Tendon rupture
Post-corticosteroid injection less than 6 weeks ago
exercises.beginnerModification: Banded wrist flexion with eccentric bias (palm-up grip, lower slowly).
exercises.evidenceFull: Tyler TF, Nicholas SJ, Schmitt BM, Mullaney M, Hogan DE. Clinical outcomes of the addition of eccentrics for rehabilitation of previously failed treatments of golfers elbow. Int J Sports Phys Ther. 2014;9(3):365-370.
exercises.evidenceSummary: Case series of 20 patients with chronic golfer's elbow who had failed prior conservative care. Reverse Tyler Twist produced significant pain reduction and functional improvement.
